question: Novelists Gerhart Hauptmann and Mario Vargas Llosa both won the Nobel Prize in Literature but in which year did Llosa most recently accomplish it?
evidence_a:

[1] He is counted among the most important promoters of literary naturalism, though he integrated other styles into his work as well.
[2] He received the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1912.
evidence_b:
TITLE: Mario Vargas Llosa
[0] Jorge Mario Pedro Vargas Llosa, 1st Marquis of Vargas Llosa (born March 28, 1936), more commonly known as Mario Vargas Llosa ( ; ] ), is a Peruvian writer, politician, journalist, essayist and college professor.
[1] Vargas Llosa is one of Latin America's most significant novelists and essayists, and one of the leading writers of his generation.
[2] Some critics consider him to have had a larger international impact and worldwide audience than any other writer of the Latin American Boom.
[3] In 2010 he won the Nobel Prize in Literature, "for his cartography of structures of power and his trenchant images of the individual's resistance, revolt, and defeat."

question: What is the former name of the only bank ine the village of Gar Munara, in Pakistan?
evidence_a:
TITLE: Gar Munara
[0] Gar Munara (Urdu: گڑھ منارہ) is a village located on the bank of the Indus River, in Swabi, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan, near the town of Marghuz.
[1] Thousands of visitors across Pakistan visit Gar Munara every year, especially in summer to enjoy the Indus River.
[2] It has a population of around 5,000.
[3] Most of the people are government workers or public servants.
[4] Many of them go abroad for work.
[5] The village's only bank, HBL, is located in the center of the village.
[6] The main crops are wheat, corn, sugarcane, and tobacco.
[7] Resorts in Gar Munara include Rahat Khan Bangla and Lewanay khan Bangla.
[8] Gar Munara village is surrounded by several other villages: Dhok to its east, Moosa Banda to its west, and Marghuz to its north, with the Indus River to its south.
[9] Cricket, kabadi and belorae are popular games in this village.
[10] The literacy rate is high in this area.
[11] There is one matriculation school for boys, and one for girls.
[12] There is also one primary school for boys and one for girls.
[13] A new college for girls was under construction and expected completion was mid-2015.
evidence_b:
TITLE: HBL Pakistan
[0] HBL (Urdu: حَبيب بينك‎ ) (formerly Habib Bank Limited) now referred to as HBL Pakistan is a Karachi based multinational bank.
[1] It is the largest bank by assets in Pakistan.

question: Are both Saritaea and Hornbeam considered the genus of plants?
evidence_a:
TITLE: Saritaea
[0] Saritaea is a monotypic genus in the trumpet-vine family, Bignoniaceae.
[1] The sole species, Saritaea magnifica, glowvine, is native to Colombia and Ecuador.
[2] The stems are almost round in cross-section, and are marked with longitudinal stripes.
[3] The 10 cm leaves have 2 leaflets and a further 2 leaflet-like appendages at the base of the leaf stalk, plus a tendril at the tip.
[4] The leaves are smooth and leathery.
[5] The plant is a very spectacular flowering, evergreen tropical climber.
[6] The large heads of showy rosy mauve to purple coloured, bell-shaped flowers 8 cm long with hairy yellow throat, borne at the end of the branches often display all year-round .
[7] When in flower it is regarded as one of the outstanding climbers of the world.
[8] Their nectar is collected by the male bees of the tropical genus "Euglossa", which pollinate the flowers by brushing against the pollen and transferring it.
[9] The fruit is a long flattened capsule containing 2-winged seeds.
evidence_b:
TITLE: Hornbeam
[0] Hornbeams are hardwood trees in the flowering plant genus "Carpinus" in the birch family Betulaceae.
[1] The 30–40 species occur across much of the temperate regions of the northern hemisphere.

question: In what city did Phog Allen serve as head basketball coach the longest?
evidence_a:
TITLE: Phog Allen
[0] Forrest Clare "Phog" Allen (November 18, 1885 – September 16, 1974) was an American basketball and baseball player, coach of American football, basketball, and baseball, college athletics administrator, and osteopathic physician.
[1] Known as the "Father of Basketball Coaching," he served as the head basketball coach at Baker University (1905–1908), the University of Kansas (1907–1909, 1919–1956), Haskell Institute—now Haskell Indian Nations University (1908–1909), and Warrensburg Teachers College—now the University of Central Missouri (1912–1919), compiling a career college basketball record of 746–264.
[2] In his 39 seasons at the helm of the Kansas Jayhawks men's basketball program, his teams won 24 conference championships and three national titles.
[3] The Helms Athletic Foundation retroactively recognized Allen's 1921–22 and 1922–23 Kansas teams as national champions.
[4] Allen's 1951–52 squad won the 1952 NCAA Tournament and his Jayhawks were runners-up in the NCAA Tournament in 1940 and 1953.
[5] His 590 wins are the most of any coach in the storied history of the Kansas basketball program.
evidence_b:
TITLE: University of Kansas
[0] The University of Kansas, often referred to as KU or Kansas, is a public research university in the U.S. state of Kansas.
[1] The main campus in Lawrence, one of the largest college towns in Kansas, is on Mount Oread, the highest elevation in Lawrence.
[2] Two branch campuses are in the Kansas City metropolitan area: the Edwards Campus in Overland Park, and the university's medical school and hospital in Kansas City.
[3] There are also educational and research sites in Parsons, Topeka, Garden City, Hays, and Leavenworth, and branches of the medical school in Wichita and Salina.
[4] The university is one of the 62 members of the Association of American Universities.

question: What caused members of Alfred Shaker Historic District move to Sabbathday Lake Shaker Village?
answer: Alfred Shakers products and goods were no longer competitive
evidence_a:
TITLE: Alfred Shaker Historic District
[0] Alfred Shaker Historic District is a historic district in Alfred, Maine, with properties on both sides of Shaker Hill Road.
[1] The area had its first Shaker "believers" in 1783 following visiting with Mother Ann Lee and became an official community starting in 1793 when a meetinghouse was built.
[2] It was home to Maine's oldest and largest Shaker community.
[3] Two notable events were the songwriting of Joseph Brackett, including "Simple Gifts", and the spiritual healing of the sick by the Shakers.
[4] When the Alfred Shakers products and goods were no longer competitive with mass-produced products and the membership had dwindled significantly, the village was closed in 1931 and members moved to Sabbathday Lake Shaker Village, also in Maine.
evidence_b:
TITLE: Sabbathday Lake Shaker Village
[0] Sabbathday Lake Shaker Village is a Shaker village near New Gloucester and Poland, Maine, in the United States.
[1] It is the last active Shaker community, with only two members as of 2017.
[2] The community was established in either 1782, 1783 or 1793, at the height of the Shaker movement in the United States.
[3] The Sabbathday Lake meetinghouse was built in 1794.
[4] The entire property was declared a National Historic Landmark in 1974.

question: What is a medium-sized French water dog, Black and Tan Virginia Foxhound or Barbet?
evidence_a:
TITLE: Black and Tan Virginia Foxhound
[0] The Black and Tan Virginia Foxhound is an American foxhound breed.
[1] It resulted of a cross breed with Bloodhounds in the 1700s.
[2] The breed was developed by the landed gentry in order to get a dog suitable for fox hunting.
[3] Besides the Black and Tan there are the Walker, Calhoun, Penn-Marydel, Goodman, July and Trigg, which all developed from similar crosses.
[4] The Black and Tan is believed to descend form hunting dogs imported to America by Robert Brooke in 1650.
[5] These hunting dogs were the ancestors of several varieties of American hounds and stayed with the Brooke family for more than 300 years.
[6] Afterwards French Foxhounds were bred in after George Washington received them as gifts from the Marquis de Lafayette.
[7] Much later, the breed's speed and stamina were improved introducing Irish Foxhounds.
evidence_b:
TITLE: Barbet (dog)
[0] The barbet is a breed of dog; it is a medium-sized French water dog.
[1] It is listed in Group 8 (retrievers, flushing dogs, water dogs) by the Société Centrale Canine, the French Kennel Club.

question: Were One Hundred and One Dalmatians and Fun and Fancy Free both animated films?
evidence_a:

[1] The 17th Disney animated feature film, the film tells the story of a litter of dalmatian puppies who are kidnapped by the villainous Cruella de Vil (Betty Lou Gerson), who wants to use their fur to make into coats.
[2] Their parents, Pongo and Perdita, (Rod Taylor and Cate Bauer respectively) set out to save their children from Cruella, all the while rescuing 84 additional puppies that were bought in pet shops, bringing the total of dalmatians to 101.
evidence_b:

[1] It is the 9th Disney animated feature film and the fourth of the package films the studio produced in the 1940s in order to save money during World War II.
[2] The Disney package films of the late 1940s helped finance "Cinderella", and subsequent others, such as "Alice in Wonderland" and "Peter Pan".

question: Which genus, Rubus or Cunonia, contains the largest number of individual species?
evidence_a:
TITLE: Rubus
[0] Rubus is a large and diverse genus of flowering plants in the rose family, Rosaceae, subfamily Rosoideae, with 250–700 species.
evidence_b:
TITLE: Cunonia
[0] Cunonia is a genus of shrubs and trees in the family Cunoniaceae.
[1] The genus has a disjunct distribution, with 24 species endemic to New Caledonia in the Pacific, and one species (Cunonia capensis) in Southern Africa.
[2] Leaves are opposite, simple or pinnate with a margin entire to serrate.
[3] Interpetiolar stipules are often conspicuous and generally enclose buds to form a spoon-like shape (hence the common name "Butterspoon Tree" for "Cunonia capensis").
[4] Flowers are bisexual, white, red (pink to purple), or green, arranged in racemes.
[5] The fruit is a capsule opening first around the base then vertically, seeds are winged.

question: The third powered aircraft built by Samuel Franklin Cody was used by the British in which World War?
evidence_a:
TITLE: Cody Circuit of Britain biplane
[0] The Cody Circuit of Britain biplane, also known as the Cody III, was the third powered aircraft built by Samuel Franklin Cody.
[1] It was flown by him in various competitions during 1911, including the Daily Mail Circuit of Britain competition in which Cody was the only British contestant to complete the course.
[2] On 29 October Cody set a new British endurance record in the aircraft, flying for five hours and fifteen minutes.
evidence_b:
TITLE: Samuel Franklin Cody
[0] Samuel Franklin Cowdery (later known as Samuel Franklin Cody; 6 March 1867 – 7 August 1913, born Davenport, Iowa, USA) was a Wild West showman and early pioneer of manned flight.
[1] He is most famous for his work on the large kites known as "Cody War-Kites", that were used by the British in World War I as a smaller alternative to balloons for artillery spotting.
[2] He was also the first man to fly an aeroplane in Britain, on 16 October 1908.
[3] A flamboyant showman, he was often confused with Buffalo Bill Cody, whose surname he took when young.

question: Which home computer was produced first, Amiga or Aamber Pegasus?
evidence_a:
TITLE: Amiga
[0] The Amiga is a family of personal computers sold by Commodore starting in 1985.
[1] The original model was part of a wave of 16 and 32 bit computers that featured 256KB or more of RAM, mouse-based GUIs, and significantly improved graphics and audio over 8-bit systems.
[2] This wave included the Atari ST—released the same year—Apple's Macintosh, and later the Apple IIGS.
[3] Based on the Motorola 68000 microprocessor, the Amiga differed from its contemporaries through the inclusion of custom hardware to accelerate graphics and sound, including sprites and a blitter, and a pre-emptive multitasking operating system called AmigaOS.

TITLE: Aamber Pegasus

[2] Their range extends from Mexico and the Caribbean to southeastern Brazil and adjacent Argentina.

question: Which gold mine has a longer history, Thompson-Lundmark Mine or Lupin Mine?

[2] Underground and shaft work began in 1939.
[3] The mine processed 133,989 tons of ore to produce 70,339 troy ounces (2188 kg) of gold and 13,782 troy ounces (429 kg) of silver.

evidence_b:
TITLE: Brooklyn Bridge
[0] The Brooklyn Bridge is a hybrid cable-stayed/suspension bridge in New York City and is one of the oldest bridges in the United States.
[1] Started in 1869 and completed fourteen (14) years later in 1883, it connects the boroughs of Manhattan and Brooklyn by spanning the East River.
[2] It has a main span of 1595.5 ft and was the first steel-wire suspension bridge constructed.
[3] It was originally called the New York and Brooklyn Bridge and the East River Bridge, but it was later dubbed the Brooklyn Bridge, a name coming from an earlier January 25, 1867, letter to the editor of the "Brooklyn Daily Eagle" and formally so named by the city government in 1915.
[4] Since opening, it has become an icon of New York City and was designated a National Historic Landmark in 1964 and a National Historic Civil Engineering Landmark in 1972.

question: In which state was the American astronomer who discovered spiral galaxy NGC 1234 born?
evidence_a:
TITLE: NGC 1234
[0] NGC 1234 is a peculiar barred spiral galaxy exhibiting a ring structure in the constellation Eridanus, discovered by Francis Preserved Leavenworth in 1886.
evidence_b:
TITLE: Francis Preserved Leavenworth
[0] Francis Preserved Leavenworth (born September 3, 1858 in Mount Vernon, Indiana; died November 12, 1928; a.k.a. "Frank Leavenworth") was an American astronomer.
[1] He discovered many New General Catalogue objects together with Frank Muller and Ormond Stone.
[2] They used a telescope with a 66-cm aperture at the Leander McCormick Observatory at the University of Virginia in Charlottesville, Virginia.

evidence_b:
TITLE: The Watcher in the Woods
[0] The Watcher in the Woods is a 1980 British-American horror film directed by John Hough, and starring Bette Davis, Carroll Baker, Lynn-Holly Johnson, Kyle Richards, and David McCallum.
[1] Based on the 1976 novel of the same name by Florence Engel Randall, the film tells the story of a teenage girl and her little sister who become encompassed in a supernatural mystery regarding a missing girl in the woods surrounding their new home in the English countryside.
